title: Engagement Packet Guide – IS3513
headerSubtitle: Section A · Section B · Section C

sections.html: Every Week 3 submission is an Engagement Packet — a professional deliverable that mirrors real consulting workflows. Three sections. One document. .docx only — no PDF submissions accepted.

sections.label: Critical Requirements:
sections.html: Format: .docx only (no PDFs)  •  Citations: 10 minimum (−10% each missing)  •  Screenshots: VM name visible in all (−5% each unverifiable)

sections.label: Section A: NEXUS Timesheet
paragraphs: Audience: NEXUS Management  •  Purpose: Billing Justification
Consulting firms bill clients for time. Every hour must be justified. Generic entries like “did lab work” won’t fly — describe what you actually did.
items: Week 1 — Internal Training: Environment setup, initial tool learning, configuration
Week 2 — Internal Training: Tool exploration, technique practice, methodology development
Week 3 — Client Billable: Engagement execution, analysis, report writing
sections.tip: Example (Module 1): Week 1: Kali VM installation (1.0 hr), network adapter config (0.5 hr), snapshot setup (0.5 hr), tool inventory (1.0 hr), recon methodology review (1.0 hr) = 4.0 hrs internal. Week 3: Brazos external recon (2.0 hr), subdomain enumeration (1.0 hr), findings analysis (1.0 hr), report drafting (1.5 hr) = 5.5 hrs billable.

sections.label: Section B: Internal Runbook
paragraphs: Audience: NEXUS Team  •  Purpose: Knowledge Transfer
Create methodology documentation so another analyst could reproduce your work. “Here’s how to do X for the next analyst.” When you leave, your methodology stays.
items: From Week 1: Environment setup steps, tool installation procedures, configuration documentation, screenshots of key steps
From Week 2: Tool usage documentation, command syntax with explanations, technique workflows, output interpretation guides
From Week 3: Engagement workflow, lessons learned, what worked and what didn’t, recommendations for next time
sections.tip: The Reproducibility Test: Could a junior analyst who wasn’t in this class follow your runbook and achieve the same results? If the answer is no, add more detail. Pro tip: If you took good screenshots in Labs X.1 and X.2, your runbook is 60–70% done before Week 3 starts. Just compile and polish.

sections.label: Section C: Client Report
paragraphs: Audience: Fictional Client Contact  •  Purpose: Professional Deliverable
Write the report you would hand to a real client. This is what Ray Jimenez, Jennifer Walsh, Amanda Nguyen, or Bobby Alamo would actually read.
items: 1. Executive Summary (1 page max) — High-level overview for non-technical readers. What did you find? What’s the risk? What should they do? No jargon. A CEO should understand this.
2. Scope and Methodology — What systems were tested? What was out of scope? What approach did you take?
3. Findings — Each finding needs: description, evidence (screenshots), severity, and business impact.
4. Risk Analysis — What’s the actual risk to the business? Likelihood, impact, and business context. Not everything high-severity is high-priority.
5. Recommendations — Actionable, prioritized, realistic. Quick wins and long-term improvements. Reference industry standards (CIS, NIST).

left.label: Writing: Do This
items: Write in third person, past tense
Use clear, professional language
Explain technical terms
Support all claims with evidence
Proofread for grammar and spelling

right.label: Writing: Avoid This
items: First person (“I ran nmap…”)
Unexplained jargon
Claims without screenshots
Copy-pasted tool output without analysis
Casual language or slang

sections.label: Pre-Submission Checklist
items: File saved as .docx — not PDF, not Pages, not Google Docs export
All three sections present: Timesheet, Runbook, Client Report
10+ citations with auto-generated bibliography (References → Bibliography)
All screenshots show VM name (kali-lastname-studentID visible)
Screenshot filenames follow ##-description.png convention
Executive summary is non-technical and under one page
Each finding includes severity, evidence, and business impact
Recommendations are actionable and reference industry standards
Document spellchecked and proofread

sections.html: Submitted to the Lab X.3 assignment in Canvas. No late work accepted. Format: .docx only.
